title: The Resolved Brief
subtitle: The 20-Minute System That Organizes Your Entire Life For The People You Love.

sections.title: Quick Snapshot
sections.subtitle: Let's see where you stand — just tap through these. No typing needed.
sections.intro_message: We'll start with a quick pulse check. These yes/no questions take about 60 seconds and show us exactly where your gaps are.

questions.prompt: Do you have a will?
questions.follow_up_tip: A basic will can be created online in about 30 minutes.

questions.prompt: When was your will last updated?
questions.follow_up_tip: Wills should be reviewed every 3-5 years or after any major life change.

questions.prompt: Have there been major life changes since then — marriage, divorce, children, property, inheritance?
questions.follow_up_tip: If yes, your will may not reflect your current wishes. Updating is usually quick and inexpensive.

questions.prompt: Where is your will physically located?
questions.placeholder: e.g. Filing cabinet in home office / Safety deposit box at Chase Bank / Attorney's office
questions.helper_text: Be specific enough that someone could find it without asking you.

questions.prompt: Who is your estate attorney? (Name + phone number)
questions.placeholder: e.g. John Smith, Smith & Associates — 214-555-0100
questions.helper_text: Leave blank if you don't have one.

questions.prompt: Do you have life insurance?
questions.follow_up_tip: Even a basic term policy provides critical protection.

questions.prompt: Do you have a health care proxy or power of attorney?
questions.follow_up_tip: This is the document that lets someone make medical decisions for you.

questions.prompt: Do you use a password manager?
questions.follow_up_tip: A password manager is the single easiest way to protect your digital life.

questions.prompt: Do you have a living will or advance directive?
questions.follow_up_tip: This tells doctors what you want if you can't speak for yourself.

questions.prompt: How many bank accounts do you have?
questions.follow_up_tip: Knowing exactly where your money sits is step one.

questions.prompt: Are your important documents organized in one place?
questions.follow_up_tip: One folder or safe with everything = peace of mind.

questions.prompt: Could your family find your insurance policies if they needed to?
questions.follow_up_tip: If the answer isn't 'easily,' we'll fix that today.

questions.prompt: Are most of your bills on autopay?
questions.follow_up_tip: Autopay keeps the lights on if you're incapacitated.

questions.prompt: Do you have any retirement accounts?
questions.follow_up_tip: 401k, IRA, Roth — your family needs to know these exist.

questions.prompt: Have you told anyone your final wishes?
questions.follow_up_tip: Even a simple conversation removes a huge burden from your family.

questions.prompt: Does anyone besides you know where your passwords are?
questions.follow_up_tip: One person needs to know — or everything gets locked out.

sections.title: Money & Accounts
sections.subtitle: Where your money lives. Just names and account types — no account numbers.
sections.intro_message: This section is all about the basics: which banks, what types of accounts, and where the paperwork is. We never ask for account numbers.

questions.prompt: Which banks or credit unions do you use?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Chase, Bank of America, local credit union...
questions.follow_up_tip: Just the names — your family needs to know where to go.

questions.prompt: Types of accounts you have?
questions.follow_up_tip: Each type may have different rules for family access.

questions.prompt: Anyone else listed on these accounts?
questions.placeholder: e.g., spouse, business partner...
questions.follow_up_tip: Joint account holders can access funds immediately.

questions.prompt: What retirement accounts do you have?
questions.follow_up_tip: Each has different rules for beneficiary payouts.

questions.prompt: Where are they held?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Fidelity, Vanguard, through employer...
questions.follow_up_tip: The institution name is all your family needs to start the process.

questions.prompt: Any investment or brokerage accounts outside retirement?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Schwab brokerage, Robinhood, real estate holdings...
questions.follow_up_tip: Include anything that holds value your family should know about.

questions.prompt: Mortgage or rent — who do you pay?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Wells Fargo mortgage, Greystar property mgmt...
questions.follow_up_tip: If something happens, this is the first bill that matters.

questions.prompt: Any loans? (car, student, personal, business)
questions.placeholder: e.g., Toyota Financial, Navient student loans...
questions.follow_up_tip: Some loans have life insurance or death discharge provisions.

questions.prompt: Credit cards you use?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Chase Sapphire, Amex, Discover...
questions.follow_up_tip: Just the card names — they'll need to be closed or transferred.

questions.prompt: Where are your financial documents kept?
questions.follow_up_tip: One central location saves your family hours of searching.

questions.prompt: Do you have a safe deposit box?
questions.follow_up_tip: Someone needs to know it exists and have the key.

questions.prompt: Which bank, and who has the key?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Chase Main St branch, spare key in desk drawer
questions.follow_up_tip: Without the key, getting into a safe deposit box requires a court order.

sections.title: Income & Bills
sections.subtitle: Where money comes in and where it goes out.
sections.intro_message: If something happened tomorrow, your family needs to know what's coming in and what's going out. This section maps that.

questions.prompt: What is your primary bank account — the one most of your money flows through?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Chase checking ending in 4521
questions.follow_up_tip: This is ground zero for your family's financial continuity.

questions.prompt: Any other accounts your family should know about?
questions.placeholder: e.g., separate savings, business account, HSA...
questions.follow_up_tip: Hidden accounts are one of the biggest surprises families face.

questions.prompt: Where does your income come from?
questions.follow_up_tip: Each source may need different steps to continue or claim.

questions.prompt: Bills on autopay?
questions.placeholder: e.g., mortgage, electric, internet, car insurance...
questions.follow_up_tip: Autopay keeps things running, but the card it's on needs to stay active.

questions.prompt: Bills you pay manually?
questions.placeholder: e.g., water bill, property taxes...
questions.follow_up_tip: These are the ones that get missed first.

questions.prompt: Subscriptions and memberships?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Netflix, gym, Amazon Prime, software...
questions.follow_up_tip: Subscriptions add up fast — your family needs to cancel what's not needed.

questions.prompt: Annual payments that could be missed?
questions.placeholder: e.g., property tax, HOA, life insurance premium, car registration...
questions.follow_up_tip: These sneak up. Missing one can trigger penalties or coverage lapses.

sections.title: Insurance & Benefits
sections.subtitle: Your safety net — what's covered and where the policies are.
sections.intro_message: Insurance is the first thing your family will need to access. Let's make sure they know what exists.

questions.prompt: Do you have life insurance?
questions.follow_up_tip: Life insurance is often the first financial lifeline for your family.

questions.prompt: Provider(s)?
questions.placeholder: e.g., MetLife through employer, Northwestern Mutual private...
questions.follow_up_tip: The provider name is all they need to start a claim.

questions.prompt: Approximate coverage amount?
questions.placeholder: e.g., $500K through work + $250K private
questions.follow_up_tip: Even a rough number helps your family plan ahead.

questions.prompt: Who is the beneficiary?
questions.placeholder: e.g., spouse, children, trust...
questions.follow_up_tip: Check this annually — life changes, beneficiaries should too.

questions.prompt: Disability insurance?
questions.follow_up_tip: Disability is more likely than death before 65. This matters.

questions.prompt: Long-term care insurance?
questions.follow_up_tip: Long-term care costs can drain a family's savings in months.

questions.prompt: Home or renter's insurance?
questions.placeholder: e.g., State Farm, policy # in filing cabinet...
questions.follow_up_tip: Needed for any property claims.

questions.prompt: Auto insurance?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Geico, Progressive...
questions.follow_up_tip: Needs to stay active on all vehicles.

questions.prompt: Any other policies? (umbrella, business, liability)
questions.placeholder: e.g., $1M umbrella through State Farm...
questions.follow_up_tip: Business policies especially can be hard to find after the fact.

questions.prompt: Where are your insurance documents?
questions.follow_up_tip: If the answer is 'not sure,' this is a great homework item.

questions.prompt: Insurance agent or broker?
questions.placeholder: e.g., John Smith, State Farm, 555-0123...
questions.follow_up_tip: An agent can help your family navigate ALL your policies at once.

sections.title: Digital Life & Access
sections.subtitle: How your family gets into your accounts when they need to.
sections.intro_message: In 2026, your digital life IS your life. If your family can't get into your phone or email, they can't do anything else.

questions.prompt: How do you manage your passwords?
questions.follow_up_tip: However you do it, one person needs to know the system.

questions.prompt: Which password manager do you use?
questions.follow_up_tip: Most password managers have emergency access features — set them up.

questions.prompt: Where will the master password or access method be stored for your family?
options: Written in The Resolved Brief envelope
questions.follow_up_tip: The Resolved Brief envelope method is simple and effective — everything in one place.

questions.prompt: Primary email account?
questions.placeholder: e.g., yourname@gmail.com
questions.follow_up_tip: Email is the skeleton key — password resets for everything go here.

questions.prompt: How would your family access this email account?
options: Password is in my password manager
I'll write it on the sealed envelope sheet
I need to figure this out
questions.follow_up_tip: Without email access, your family can't reset passwords for any other account. This is the single most important access item to document. Add it to your sealed envelope.

questions.prompt: Could your family get into your phone if needed?
options: No — only I can unlock it
questions.follow_up_tip: Phone access unlocks 2FA codes, banking apps, and more.

questions.prompt: Will you add your phone passcode to your sealed envelope?
questions.follow_up_tip: Your phone is the key to everything — banking apps, 2FA codes, photos, and account recovery. Add your passcode to the fillable sheet and seal it in your envelope.

questions.prompt: Could your family get into your computer?
options: No — only I can access it
questions.follow_up_tip: Tax documents, financial software, and saved files often live here.

questions.prompt: Financial apps on your phone?
questions.follow_up_tip: Each app may have its own login and 2FA.

questions.prompt: Do you have any cryptocurrency?
questions.follow_up_tip: Crypto without a recovery phrase is gone forever.

questions.prompt: Which platform, and where is the recovery phrase stored?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Coinbase, seed phrase in The Resolved Brief envelope...
questions.follow_up_tip: This is arguably the most important single item in your entire Resolved Brief.

questions.prompt: Where are your photos stored?
questions.follow_up_tip: Photos are irreplaceable. Make sure at least one backup exists.

questions.prompt: Important documents in cloud storage?
questions.follow_up_tip: Tax returns, contracts, and legal docs — your family will need these.

sections.title: Medical & Emergency
sections.subtitle: Who makes decisions and what they need to know.
sections.intro_message: This section covers the hard but important stuff — who speaks for you if you can't, and what doctors need to know.

questions.prompt: Who should make medical decisions if you can't?
questions.placeholder: e.g., spouse, parent, sibling, friend...
questions.follow_up_tip: This should match your health care proxy document.

questions.prompt: Does this person know they've been chosen?
questions.follow_up_tip: Don't surprise someone with this responsibility. Have the conversation.

questions.prompt: Backup person if they're unavailable?
questions.placeholder: e.g., sibling, close friend...
questions.follow_up_tip: Always have a backup — your first choice might be traveling or unreachable.

questions.prompt: Current medical conditions?
questions.placeholder: e.g., high blood pressure, diabetes, asthma...
questions.follow_up_tip: ER doctors need this information fast.

questions.prompt: Current medications?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Lisinopril 10mg, Metformin 500mg...
questions.follow_up_tip: Include dosages if you know them.

questions.prompt: Allergies (medications, foods, other)?
questions.placeholder: e.g., penicillin, shellfish, latex...
questions.follow_up_tip: Allergies can be life-threatening — this info saves lives.

questions.prompt: Primary care doctor?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Dr. Smith, Northwestern Medical...
questions.follow_up_tip: Your PCP has your complete medical history.

questions.prompt: Specialists you see regularly?
questions.placeholder: e.g., cardiologist Dr. Jones, therapist Dr. Lee...
questions.follow_up_tip: Include any regular appointments your family should know about.

questions.prompt: Health insurance provider?
questions.placeholder: e.g., Blue Cross Blue Shield through employer...
questions.follow_up_tip: Hospital billing needs this immediately.

questions.prompt: Where is your insurance card?
questions.follow_up_tip: A photo on a shared cloud folder is the safest backup.

questions.prompt: Do you have a living will or advance directive?
questions.follow_up_tip: This document tells doctors what you want when you can't tell them yourself.

questions.prompt: Resuscitation preference?
options: Want to discuss with doctor first
questions.follow_up_tip: There's no wrong answer — but having an answer prevents family conflict.

questions.prompt: Organ donor?
questions.follow_up_tip: Make sure this matches what's on your driver's license.

sections.title: Wishes & Final Instructions
sections.subtitle: The things only you can tell them.
sections.intro_message: This is the most personal section. Take your time. Skip anything that feels too heavy — you can always come back.

questions.prompt: Do you prefer burial or cremation?
questions.follow_up_tip: Having this answered removes one of the hardest decisions from your family.

questions.prompt: Any specific wishes about your remains?
questions.placeholder: e.g., family plot, scatter ashes at the lake, specific funeral home...
questions.follow_up_tip: Even 'no preference' is helpful — it gives your family permission to choose.

questions.prompt: What kind of service would you want?
questions.follow_up_tip: Your family wants to honor you the way you'd want.

questions.prompt: Any specific requests?
questions.placeholder: e.g., play a certain song, specific readings, no flowers — donate instead...
questions.follow_up_tip: These details make the service feel like YOU.

questions.prompt: Anyone who should be notified that your family might not think of?
questions.placeholder: e.g., old college friend, former colleague, online community...
questions.follow_up_tip: There are always people who slip through the cracks.

questions.prompt: Specific items for specific people?
questions.placeholder: e.g., grandmother's ring to daughter, guitar to best friend...
questions.follow_up_tip: This prevents conflict and ensures your wishes are honored.

questions.prompt: Anything to donate, sell, or destroy?
questions.placeholder: e.g., donate clothes to Goodwill, sell the boat, shred old journals...
questions.follow_up_tip: Give your family clear permission to handle things.

questions.prompt: Is there anything you want your family to know — messages, values, traditions, love you want to pass on?
questions.placeholder: This is your space. Say what matters most to you.
questions.follow_up_tip: This is the page your family will read over and over.
